name: Compound Interest

sourceRefs.label: 2022_q1 page 63 Q193-194 (SI-vs-CI difference shortcut; direct CI calculation, with answer key)
2025_q1 page 79 Q141 (find principal from two CI amounts at different years, with answer key)
2024_q page 31 Q45 (successive-percentage-change/depreciation problem, with answer key — found while researching an earlier topic, stashed and used here)

researchNotes: Strong real evidence — 4 confirmed hits across 2022/2024/2025 covering 4 distinct shapes: the SI-vs-CI difference shortcut formula, direct CI calculation, working backward from two CI amounts to find the principal, and successive-percentage-change problems (which are CI in disguise — depreciation/appreciation year over year is mathematically identical to compound interest with a negative or mixed rate). Expanded 2026-07 pass (append-only — also used Day 7, already completed): added half-yearly/quarterly compounding (a frequently tested variant the original pass omitted), the generalized 'x% increase then x% decrease never nets to zero' principle, and more successive-percentage-change practice. Every question, new and pre-existing, now carries the mandatory Part-B `trick`. All arithmetic re-verified by hand. Round-2 pass (2026-07, second expansion): added the 3-year CI formula/shortcut (extending the existing 2-year-only coverage) and a 6-month quarterly-compounding example. Caught and fixed one arithmetic mismatch of my own in an early draft of m18 (options didn't include the actual computed value) before finalizing.

reading.intro: Compound Interest is 1 of 10 named Aptitude sub-skills. Real exam evidence shows TNPSC tests the standard CI formula, a useful SI-vs-CI shortcut, AND disguises CI as 'successive percentage change' word problems (depreciation, year-over-year growth) — recognize all three shapes.

qa.question: Formula: What is the Compound Interest formula, and the shortcut for the DIFFERENCE between SI and CI over 2 years?
qa.answer: Amount = P(1+R/100)^T, so CI = Amount − P. For exactly 2 years, there's a shortcut: CI − SI = P×(R/100)². This avoids computing CI and SI separately when you only need their difference.

qa.question: Real exam (2022): Find the difference between SI and CI for 2 years on a principal of Rs.5,000 at 8% per annum.
Real exam (2022): Find the compound interest on Rs.5,000 at 12% per annum for 2 years, compounded annually.
Real exam (2025): A sum becomes Rs.13,380 after 3 years and Rs.20,070 after 6 years on compound interest. Find the original sum.
qa.answer: The amount after 6 years equals the amount after 3 years multiplied by the SAME growth factor again (since compounding is multiplicative): (1+R/100)³ = 20070/13380 = 1.5. The sum itself: P = Amount-after-3-years ÷ (1+R/100)³ = 13380 ÷ 1.5 = Rs.8,920.

qa.question: Real exam (2024): A motorcycle's value 2 years ago was Rs.80,000. It depreciates 4% in the first year, then increases 4% in the second year. Find the present value.
qa.answer: Apply each year's change as its own multiplier, in sequence — this is compound interest with a changing/mixed rate: 80,000 × 0.96 (first year, −4%) × 1.04 (second year, +4%) = 80,000 × 0.9984 = Rs.79,872. Note the result is slightly LESS than the original Rs.80,000 — a decrease followed by an equal-percentage increase never fully cancels out, since the increase applies to a smaller base.

qa.question: How do you adjust the compound interest formula for half-yearly or quarterly compounding?
qa.answer: Divide the annual rate by the number of compounding periods per year (n), and multiply the number of years by n: Amount = P×(1+(R/n)/100)^(n×T). Half-yearly: n=2 (halve the rate, double the exponent). Quarterly: n=4 (quarter the rate, quadruple the exponent).
qa.tip: Half-yearly compounding always beats the same nominal annual rate compounded yearly, since interest starts earning interest sooner — a useful sanity check on your answer's direction.

qa.question: Find the compound interest on Rs.10,000 for 1 year at 10% per annum, compounded half-yearly.
qa.answer: Half-yearly rate = 5%, 2 periods in 1 year. Amount = 10,000×(1.05)² = 10,000×1.1025 = Rs.11,025. CI = Rs.1,025 (slightly more than the Rs.1,000 you'd get compounding annually just once, since interest compounds twice within the year).

qa.question: What is the general principle when a quantity increases by x% and then decreases by the SAME x%?
qa.answer: It never returns to the original value — the net result is always a decrease of x²/100 percent. This is because the two changes are multipliers (1+x/100) and (1−x/100), whose product is 1−(x/100)², always slightly less than 1. E.g., +20% then −20% nets to a 4% decrease (20²/100=4); +25% then −25% nets to a 6.25% decrease.

qa.question: How do 'successive percentage change' word problems (population growth, price hikes, depreciation) relate to compound interest?
qa.answer: They ARE compound interest in disguise — each period's percentage change is applied as a multiplier to the previous period's value, exactly like annual compounding. Recognizing this lets you reuse the same multiplicative-chaining technique regardless of whether the problem says 'interest', 'population', or 'value'.

qa.question: A city's population grows 5% in year 1 and 8% in year 2 from a base of 40,000. Find the population after 2 years.
Round-2 pass — What is the compound interest formula and shortcut for 3 years (rather than 2)?
qa.answer: For 3 years, CI = P×((1+R/100)³−1) directly from the base formula. There's also a difference shortcut: CI−SI over 3 years = P×(R/100)²×(3+R/100) — less commonly needed than the 2-year version, but useful when a problem specifically asks for the SI-CI gap over 3 years rather than the CI itself.

qa.question: Find the compound interest on Rs.10,000 at 10% per annum for 3 years, compounded annually.
Find the compound interest on Rs.8,000 for 9 months at 20% per annum, compounded quarterly.
qa.answer: Quarterly rate = 20/4 = 5%. Number of quarters in 9 months = 3. Amount = 8,000×(1.05)³ = 8,000×1.157625 = Rs.9,261. CI = 9,261−8,000 = Rs.1,261.
qa.tip: For quarterly compounding, divide the annual rate by 4 and count the number of QUARTERS (not years) as the exponent — 9 months = 3 quarters exactly, a clean case.

questions.prompt: Find the difference between SI and CI for 2 years on a principal of Rs.8,000 at 5% per annum.
questions.trick: CI−SI over 2 years = P×(R/100)² directly — no need to compute SI and CI separately.

questions.prompt: Find the compound interest on Rs.10,000 at 10% per annum for 2 years, compounded annually.
questions.trick: CI = P×((1+R/100)²−1) for 2 years — plug directly rather than computing each year's interest separately.

questions.prompt: A sum becomes Rs.16,000 after 2 years and Rs.19,360 after 4 years on compound interest. Find the original sum.
questions.trick: Growth factor = later amount ÷ earlier amount (for equal-length gaps); divide the earlier checkpoint by that factor to recover the original Principal.

questions.prompt: A machine valued at Rs.50,000 depreciates by 10% in the first year and a further 10% in the second year. Find its value after 2 years.
questions.trick: Apply each year's percentage change as its own multiplier in sequence — never subtract percentages across multiple years directly.

questions.prompt: Find the compound interest on Rs.20,000 at 15% per annum for 2 years, compounded annually.
questions.trick: CI = P×((1+R/100)²−1) — the same direct 2-year formula.

questions.prompt: Find the compound interest on Rs.8,000 for 1 year at 10% per annum, compounded half-yearly.
questions.trick: For half-yearly compounding, halve the annual rate and double the number of periods before applying the formula.

questions.prompt: Find the difference between SI and CI for 2 years on Rs.12,000 at 5% per annum.
questions.trick: CI−SI (2 years) = P×(R/100)² — the same direct shortcut every time.

questions.prompt: A population grows 10% in year 1 and 10% in year 2 from a base of 50,000. Find the population after 2 years.
questions.trick: Chain each period's growth multiplier and apply once to the base — this is CI in disguise.

questions.prompt: A machine worth Rs.40,000 depreciates 5% in the first year and 5% in the second year. Find its value after 2 years.
questions.trick: Multiply by (1−rate) for each depreciating year in sequence, exactly like compound interest with a negative rate.

questions.prompt: Find the compound interest on Rs.15,000 at 20% per annum for 2 years, compounded annually.
questions.trick: CI = P×((1+R/100)²−1) — plug directly for 2-year, whole-number-rate problems.

questions.prompt: A sum becomes Rs.12,100 after 2 years and Rs.14,641 after 4 years on compound interest. Find the original sum.
questions.trick: Divide the earlier checkpoint amount by the growth factor for that same duration to recover the original Principal.

questions.prompt: If a compound interest problem just says 'compounded annually' without specifying frequency further, how many compounding periods occur per year?
questions.explanation: Once per year (n=1) is the default unless stated otherwise.
questions.trick: Never assume half-yearly/quarterly compounding unless the problem explicitly says so — 'annually' or unstated always means once per year.

questions.prompt: Find the compound interest on Rs.20,000 for 1 year at 8% per annum, compounded half-yearly.
questions.trick: Halve the rate, double the periods — the standard half-yearly adjustment.

questions.prompt: A quantity increases by 20% and then decreases by 20%. What is the net percentage change?
questions.trick: +x% then −x% always nets to a decrease of x²/100 percent — the decrease applies to a larger base than the increase did, so it never fully cancels.

questions.prompt: Find the compound interest on Rs.25,000 at 8% per annum for 2 years, compounded annually.
questions.trick: CI = P×((1+R/100)²−1) — the same 2-year formula, plugged directly.

questions.prompt: A quantity increases by 25% and then decreases by 25%. Find the net percentage change.
Find the compound interest on Rs.12,000 at 10% per annum for 3 years, compounded annually.
questions.trick: For 3 years, CI = P×((1+R/100)³−1) — the 3-year extension of the 2-year direct formula.

questions.prompt: Find the compound interest on Rs.16,000 for 6 months at 20% per annum, compounded quarterly.
questions.trick: 6 months at quarterly compounding = 2 quarters — halve the rate to 5% and use exponent 2, not 0.5.

questions.prompt: Find the compound interest on Rs.20,000 at 5% per annum for 3 years, compounded annually.
questions.trick: CI = P×((1+R/100)³−1) for any 3-year annual-compounding problem.
